skill.name: frontend-dev-guidelines
skill.description: Guidelines for building React and TypeScript frontends with Suspense, lazy loading, TanStack Query, TanStack Router, MUI v7, feature-based folders, and performance patterns.
skill.summary: Build consistent React and TypeScript frontends with modern architecture patterns.
skill.author: BrianDai22

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown guidance and TypeScript examples, not executable skill behavior. No prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, shell execution, or malicious network behavior was found. The skill is safe to publish with low residual risk from example code that developers must adapt to their own projects.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Matches Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The external command detections are caused by Markdown code fences and inline code examples, not Ruby backtick execution or shell invocation. The reviewed lines show React, TypeScript, imports, hooks, and checklist syntax with no executable command runner.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation syntax and TypeScript examples. No shell API, child process call, or executable script file was found at these locations.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Matches Are Frontend Code-Splitting Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The dynamic import detections document React.lazy and on-demand library loading for frontend bundle optimization. They do not load remote code, compute import paths from untrusted input, or execute marketplace install hooks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use static local module names such as component paths and known libraries. The semantic context is performance guidance, not script execution.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Matches Are Relative Import Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The filesystem detections occur in examples that compare preferred aliases with relative imports or import local feature files. No file reading, file writing, directory traversal, or user-controlled path handling is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged sequences are TypeScript module specifiers inside documentation. There is no filesystem API use and no input-controlled path construction.

low_findings.title: Network Matches Are Local Application API Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The network detections describe use of an existing application API client and a fetch cancellation example. No external collection endpoint, credential forwarding, telemetry, or hidden network behavior appears in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use relative API paths and a project apiClient abstraction. A semantic sweep found no exfiltration terms, secret access, or unauthorized destination.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Hits Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Representative high and low blocker hits point to prose, headings, JSX examples, spacing shorthand, or ordinary TypeScript syntax. No MD5, SHA1, cryptographic API, host reconnaissance command, or system inventory behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations do not contain cryptographic calls or reconnaissance commands. They are plain documentation text and frontend style examples.

content.value_statement: Frontend projects often drift into mixed patterns for data, routing, styling, and file structure. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concrete React and TypeScript conventions to follow.
actual_capabilities: Guides React component structure with typed props, named exports, and lazy loading patterns.
Recommends Suspense and useSuspenseQuery patterns for new data-driven components.
Defines feature-based folder organization for API services, components, hooks, helpers, and types.
Documents MUI v7 styling conventions, including sx usage and Grid size syntax.
Provides TanStack Router examples for folder-based routes, loaders, and lazy route components.
Covers performance practices such as memoization, callback stability, and dynamic imports.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not an automated lint rule or code generator.
The conventions are tailored to React, TypeScript, MUI, TanStack Query, and TanStack Router projects.
Some examples assume project-specific aliases such as @/, ~types, ~components, and ~features.
Teams should adapt API route names and folder names to their own backend and product domains.

use_cases.title: Create a New Feature Module
use_cases.description: Use the feature folder, API service, hook, type, and component patterns when adding a new product area.

use_cases.title: Standardize Existing React Code
use_cases.description: Compare existing components against the documented Suspense, routing, styling, and export conventions.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests Faster
use_cases.description: Use the checklists to identify inconsistent loading states, imports, file placement, and TypeScript usage.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Component
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single component.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React TypeScript component for [purpose]. Follow the frontend-dev-guidelines patterns for props, exports, loading states, imports, and MUI styling.

prompt_templates.title: Add Data Fetching
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a data-driven view.
prompt_templates.prompt: Add data fetching for [entity] using the skill guidelines. Use useSuspenseQuery, a feature API service, typed responses, and Suspense boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Feature Folder
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a complete feature structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design the folder structure and key files for a [feature name] feature. Follow the feature-based organization, API, hooks, types, and public export rules.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Frontend Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this frontend flow against the skill guidelines. Identify changes for Suspense, TanStack Query, route setup, MUI v7 styling, imports, and TypeScript safety.

output_examples.input: Create a user profile component that loads user data.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan using a typed props interface, a Suspense query hook, a feature API method, MUI layout, and a Suspense boundary.
A feature folder plan with API services, components, hooks, helpers, local types, and a public export file.

output_examples.input: Review this page for loading-state issues.
output_examples.output: A review summary that flags early loading returns, recommends SuspenseLoader boundaries, and explains how to preserve layout stability.

best_practices: Use the skill before creating new React components, routes, or feature folders.
Align project aliases and API client names before applying examples directly.
Ask for a review against the guidelines when refactoring older loading or data-fetching code.
anti_patterns: Do not use it as a substitute for project-specific security, accessibility, or testing review.
Do not copy route paths or API names without adapting them to the actual application.
Do not apply Suspense patterns to legacy areas without checking current error boundaries and query setup.

faq.question: What frameworks does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets React and TypeScript projects using MUI v7, TanStack Query, and TanStack Router.

faq.question: Can it create production-ready code by itself?
faq.answer: It can guide implementation, but developers still need to test behavior, accessibility, and project integration.

faq.question: Does it require the listed import aliases?
faq.answer: No. The aliases are examples from the source project and should be mapped to the local project setup.

faq.question: Is this useful for existing codebases?
faq.answer: Yes. It is useful for reviews and refactors where teams want consistent frontend patterns.

faq.question: Does it include backend guidance?
faq.answer: No. It only describes frontend API service patterns and assumes an existing backend contract.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
